profile.behavior: Intelligent, often wary woodland corvid that forages in trees and on the ground. Omnivorous, with a strong habit of collecting and burying acorns; also eats other seeds, fruit, invertebrates, eggs, and small vertebrates. Usually resident but may disperse when food crops fail. Gives harsh, rasping alarm calls and readily mimics other sounds.
field_marks: Stocky, medium-sized corvid with broad, rounded wings and a long black tail
Variable crown, often pale with black streaking, above a bold black moustache stripe
Bright azure-blue shoulder panel finely barred with black
Strong black-and-white wing pattern and a conspicuous white rump
Stout black bill, pale grayish eye, and sturdy pinkish-brown legs
profile.habitat: Woodland and forest with dense tree and shrub cover, especially oak-rich mixed or deciduous woods; also coniferous forest, wooded parks, orchards, and large gardens. Usually avoids extensive open ground.

sources.title: Birds of the World — Eurasian Jay: Plumages, Molts, and Structure

findings: Species identity is correct: the plate depicts Eurasian Jay (Garrulus glandarius), family Corvidae.

The main bird has one head, one beak, two wings (the far wing is naturally obscured in the profile pose), two legs, and one tail; no duplicated or malformed anatomy is evident. The detached wing and head at the bottom are clearly labeled-style detail studies, not extra anatomy on the bird.

Plumage is broadly accurate: pinkish-rufous body, pale black-streaked crown, bold black moustache, pale grayish iris, stout dark bill, black tail, white rump, and contrasting black, white, and blue folded-wing pattern agree with the references and live sources.

Minor species-detail issue: the main illustration's blue-and-black barred covert panel is substantially oversized and elongated downward. Field photographs show a smaller, more compact shoulder/upperwing patch; the detached wing study repeats the oversized treatment.

Minor species-detail issue: the folded-wing pattern is somewhat simplified, with unusually broad white and blue sections compared with the more compact, complex pattern in the reference photographs. These do not prevent confident identification.

All printed taxonomy, measurements, habitat, behavior, diet, vocalization, and field-mark statements are supported. Birds of the World gives length 32–35 cm, wingspan 52–58 cm, and subspecies-level masses spanning 102–204 g.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
The layout is clear, legible, balanced, and appropriate for a scientific field-journal plate; the main illustration, detail studies, text, palette, and scale-like margin do not overlap materially.

verification_sources.title: Birds of the World — Eurasian Jay: Plumages, Molts, and Structure
